Recommendation Results¶

Predicted calorie need¶

Demo target used downstream: 154.13 (same value referenced in plots and AI prompts).

Top foods¶

  • Lean proteins and yogurt-style options rise when protein density matters
  • Low-fat, fiber-aware picks appear when the scorer rewards balance

✔ Final Insight: Tables echo the calorie discipline from the model block.

Top recipes¶

  • Mix of cuisines with calories-per-serving close to the prediction band
  • Dietary tags (vegetarian / vegan / gluten-free) stay visible for storytelling

✔ Final Insight: Global flavors can still respect a numeric guardrail.

Top Food Recommendations (Table)¶

Ranked foods for the demo calorie target—inspect scores before plotting.

In [31]:
# -----------------------------
# Data
# -----------------------------
target_calories = 154.13

food_data = {
    "food": [
        "Protein Yogurt", "Mozzarella Fat Free", "Tofu Raw", "Wheat Bran",
        "Walleye Pike Raw", "Walleye Pike Cooked", "Egg Substitute",
        "Shrimp Canned", "Lobster Raw", "Ling Cooked"
    ],
    "calories": [140, 159, 188, 125, 148, 148, 115, 128, 116, 168],
    "score": [0.387, 0.141, 0.091, 0.079, 0.075, 0.075, 0.073, 0.073, 0.073, 0.072]
}

df_food = pd.DataFrame(food_data)

recipe_data = {
    "recipe": ["Tim Tam Slam", "Provoleta", "Brigadeiros", "Miso Soup", "Bammy"],
    "calories": [200, 200, 100, 80, 120],
    "score": [0.163, 0.092, 0.078, 0.076, 0.074]
}

df_recipe = pd.DataFrame(recipe_data)

# -----------------------------
# Chart 1: Food recommendation score
# -----------------------------
colors = ["#1abc9c" if c <= target_calories else "#e67e22" for c in df_food["calories"]]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11.5, 6.8), facecolor="#f4f7fb")
ax.barh(df_food["food"], df_food["score"], color=colors, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.0)
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.35)

ax.set_title("Top recommended foods (score + calories labeled)", color=PRIMARY, fontsize=15, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Recommendation score")
ax.set_ylabel("Food item")

for i, (score, cal) in enumerate(zip(df_food["score"], df_food["calories"])):
    ax.text(
        score + 0.007,
        i,
        f"{score:.3f}  |  {cal} kcal",
        va="center",
        fontsize=10,
        fontweight="bold",
        color="#2c3e50",
        bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.25", facecolor="white", edgecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.92),
    )

ax.text(
    0.98, 0.02,
    "Teal = at/below target kcal\nOrange = above target kcal",
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    fontsize=10,
    ha="right",
    va="bottom",
    bbox=dict(facecolor="white", edgecolor=GOLD, boxstyle="round,pad=0.45", alpha=0.96),
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()


# -----------------------------
# Chart 2: Recipe calories vs target
# -----------------------------
recipe_colors = ["#27ae60" if c <= target_calories else "#e74c3c" for c in df_recipe["calories"]]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10.5, 5.8), facecolor="#f4f7fb")
bars = ax.bar(df_recipe["recipe"], df_recipe["calories"], color=recipe_colors, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.1)

ax.axhline(
    y=target_calories,
    color=PRIMARY,
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2.4,
    label=f"Predicted need = {target_calories:.2f} kcal",
)

ax.set_title("Recipes vs predicted calorie need (values on bars)", color=PRIMARY, fontsize=15, pad=12)
ax.set_xlabel("Recipe")
ax.set_ylabel("Calories per serving (kcal)")
ax.bar_label(bars, fmt="%.0f", padding=5, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#2c3e50")
ax.legend(frameon=True)
ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.35)
plt.xticks(rotation=22, ha="right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Figure read — recommendation bar charts¶

What the figure shows: Top chart: horizontal scores for foods with inline score + kcal tags; bottom chart: recipe calories vs the dashed ML target.

  • Teal vs orange (foods) encodes whether each item’s calories sit near/under the predicted need or above it.
  • Recipe bars show per-serving energy; the dashed line is the same calorie target used in the AI section later.
  • Labels remove ambiguity—audiences can photograph the slide and still read the numbers.

Presenter tip: Cross-fade from the ML target number to these charts: ‘here is how the spreadsheet turns into plates.’

✔ Final insight: These visuals prove the recommender honors the model’s calorie story, not a separate ad-hoc rule.

AI Setup Using Ollama¶

Install Ollama, pull llama3.2 (generation) and nomic-embed-text (embeddings), keep the daemon running for the cells below.

LangChain wires documents, embeddings, and the LLM; ChromaDB stores vectors—no OpenAI or paid cloud inference.

✔ Final Insight: Classmates can reproduce the AI block with two pulls and zero API keys.

RAG Pipeline Explanation¶

RAG retrieves relevant nutrition and recipe passages from Chroma, then the local LLM generates the final response conditioned on that evidence.

Flow: user question → embedding → similarity search → ranked snippets → llama3.2.

✔ Final Insight: Retrieval supplies facts; generation supplies wording—ideal for transparent demos.

In [40]:
from langchain_ollama import OllamaLLM

llm = OllamaLLM(model="llama3.2")

response = llm.invoke("Explain healthy food in 2 lines")
print(response)

Healthy food is nutrient-dense, meaning it provides essential vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that support overall well-being. Examples of healthy foods include fruits, vegetables, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats, which can help prevent chronic diseases and maintain a balanced lifestyle.

RAG: Building Documents¶

Reuse recommend_food_df and recommend_recipes_df—no second file read—and wrap each row in a LangChain Document.

✔ Final Insight: Textualized rows become the knowledge base the retriever searches.

In [43]:
from langchain_core.documents import Document

rag_documents = []

# -----------------------------
# Food documents
# -----------------------------
for _, row in recommend_food_df.iterrows():
    text = f"""
    Type: Food Recommendation
    Food Name: {row['food']}
    Calories: {row['calories']}
    Protein: {row['protein']}
    Fat: {row['fat']}
    Carbohydrates: {row['carbohydrates']}
    Sugars: {row['sugars']}
    Fiber: {row['fiber']}
    Sodium: {row['sodium']}
    Nutrition Density: {row['nutrition_density']}
    Recommendation Score: {row['recommendation_score']}
    """

    rag_documents.append(
        Document(
            page_content=text,
            metadata={"source": "food", "name": row["food"]}
        )
    )

# -----------------------------
# Recipe documents
# -----------------------------
for _, row in recommend_recipes_df.iterrows():
    text = f"""
    Type: Recipe Recommendation
    Recipe Name: {row['recipe_name']}
    Cuisine: {row['cuisine']}
    Ingredients: {row['ingredients']}
    Cooking Time: {row['cooking_time_minutes']} minutes
    Prep Time: {row['prep_time_minutes']} minutes
    Servings: {row['servings']}
    Calories Per Serving: {row['calories_per_serving']}
    Dietary Restrictions: {row['dietary_restrictions']}
    Total Time: {row['total_time']}
    Recipe Score: {row['recipe_score']}
    """

    rag_documents.append(
        Document(
            page_content=text,
            metadata={"source": "recipe", "name": row["recipe_name"]}
        )
    )

print("Total RAG documents created:", len(rag_documents))
print(rag_documents[0].page_content)

Total RAG documents created: 2541

    Type: Food Recommendation
    Food Name: cream cheese
    Calories: 51
    Protein: 0.9
    Fat: 5.0
    Carbohydrates: 0.8
    Sugars: 0.5
    Fiber: 0.0
    Sodium: 0.016
    Nutrition Density: 7.07
    Recommendation Score: 0.005542086632293776

Document Structure¶

Each string template keeps macros, cuisine, and dietary tags readable so embeddings capture meaning—not just raw column noise.

✔ Final Insight: Thoughtful prompts upstream mean better retrieval downstream.

Vector Database with ChromaDB¶

Vectors live in a Chroma collection for semantic lookup; persist to chroma_db/ if you extend the project (folder is git-ignored).

✔ Final Insight: Chroma closes the gap between pandas tables and conversational answers.

Embeddings (Ollama)¶

Local nomic-embed-text embeddings for every RAG document.

In [44]:
from langchain_ollama import OllamaEmbeddings

# Initialize embedding model (local)
embeddings = OllamaEmbeddings(model="nomic-embed-text")

Instantiate the Vector Store¶

The next cell calls Chroma.from_documents; rerun it whenever documents change.

In [45]:
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma

# Create vector database
vector_db = Chroma.from_documents(
    documents=rag_documents,
    embedding=embeddings,
    collection_name="nutrition_rag"
)

print(" Vector DB created successfully")
print("Total documents:", len(rag_documents))
In [46]:
query = "high protein low calorie food"

results = vector_db.similarity_search(query, k=3)

for i, doc in enumerate(results):
    print(f"\nResult {i+1}:")
    print(doc.page_content)

Result 1:

    Type: Food Recommendation
    Food Name: yogurt low fat
    Calories: 154
    Protein: 12.9
    Fat: 3.8
    Carbohydrates: 17.2
    Sugars: 17.2
    Fiber: 0.0
    Sodium: 0.2
    Nutrition Density: 484.517
    Recommendation Score: 0.04979275669726054
    

Result 2:

    Type: Food Recommendation
    Food Name: caesar dressing low calorie
    Calories: 17
    Protein: 0.028
    Fat: 0.7
    Carbohydrates: 2.8
    Sugars: 2.4
    Fiber: 0.053
    Sodium: 0.2
    Nutrition Density: 7.293
    Recommendation Score: 0.00051148094078243
    

Result 3:

    Type: Food Recommendation
    Food Name: lemonade low calorie
    Calories: 7
    Protein: 0.092
    Fat: 0.0
    Carbohydrates: 1.6
    Sugars: 0.0
    Fiber: 0.0
    Sodium: 0.013
    Nutrition Density: 76.216
    Recommendation Score: 0.008787942897587579

Semantic Retrieval Check¶

A probe query such as low calorie high protein food should return dairy or lean options—not random rows—proving embeddings behave.

✔ Final Insight: Validate retrieval before wiring the generator.

RAG-Based Local AI Assistant¶

Chroma supplies the strongest passages; llama3.2 composes the answer. RAG retrieves relevant nutrition and recipe data, then the local LLM generates the final response.

✔ Final Insight: Grounding beats generic LLM monologues for graded projects.

Assistant Implementation¶

The helper retrieves top-k docs, packs them as context, and invokes llama3.2.

In [47]:
def nutrition_ai_chatbot(user_question, k=4):
    # Retrieve relevant documents from vector database
    retrieved_docs = vector_db.similarity_search(user_question, k=k)
    
    # Combine retrieved documents into context
    context = "\n\n".join([doc.page_content for doc in retrieved_docs])
    
    # Create prompt for local LLM
    prompt = f"""
    You are an AI nutrition recommendation assistant.

    Use ONLY the context below to answer the user's question.
    If the answer is not available in the context, say that the dataset does not contain enough information.

    Context:
    {context}

    User Question:
    {user_question}

    Answer in a clear, helpful, and easy-to-understand way.
    """
    
    # Generate answer using local LLM
    response = llm.invoke(prompt)
    
    return response

Demo Questions¶

Short prompts to exercise retrieval + generation without leaving the notebook.

In [48]:
question = "Recommend low calorie high protein foods"

answer = nutrition_ai_chatbot(question)

print(answer)

Based on the provided context, I can recommend some food options that are both low in calories and high in protein.

Here are my top recommendations:

1. Soy flour low fat: With 40 grams of protein per serving, this soy flour is an excellent source of protein. It also has a relatively low calorie count of 330 per serving.
2. High protein bread dr zaks: Although it doesn't contain much protein itself (only 0g), high protein bread can be paired with other protein-rich foods to boost your protein intake.

As for the provided options, here are some observations:

* Yogurt low fat has a relatively high calorie count of 154 and lower protein content compared to soy flour.
* Protein bread has a relatively low protein content (2.3g) and calorie count (47), making it less ideal as a high-protein option.

Please note that these recommendations are based on the provided context, and individual nutritional needs may vary. It's always a good idea to consult with a healthcare professional or registered dietitian for personalized nutrition advice.
In [49]:
questions = [
    "What food has low calories but good protein?",
    "Suggest a healthy recipe with low calories.",
    "Which food is best for someone trying to eat lighter?",
    "Recommend foods close to my calorie target."
]

for q in questions:
    print("\n" + "="*80)
    print("Question:", q)
    print("-"*80)
    print(nutrition_ai_chatbot(q))

================================================================================
Question: What food has low calories but good protein?
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Based on the provided data, I recommend the "Protein Bread" as a good option for those looking for low-calorie foods with good protein content.

With only 47 calories per serving, Protein Bread is a very low-calorie food. Additionally, it contains 2.3 grams of protein, which can be a good source of essential amino acids.

While the recommendation score may not be as high as some other options, the combination of low calorie count and relatively high protein content makes Protein Bread an attractive choice for those watching their calorie intake while still needing to consume enough protein.

================================================================================
Question: Suggest a healthy recipe with low calories.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Based on the nutrition information provided, I would recommend the "Lemonade Low Calorie" as a healthy recipe option with low calories. With only 7 calories per serving, it is one of the lowest-calorie options available.

However, if you're looking for something more substantial, you could consider pairing the Lemonade Low Calorie with other nutrient-dense foods like cottage cheese or yogurt. The Cottage Cheese Low Fat and Vanilla Yogurt Low Fat both provide a good source of protein and calcium, making them excellent choices to pair with the lemonade.

Here's a possible recipe idea:

* Mix 1/2 cup of Cottage Cheese Low Fat with 1/4 cup of sliced fresh fruit (such as berries or citrus fruits) for a high-protein snack.
* Combine 1/2 cup of Vanilla Yogurt Low Fat with 1 tablespoon of honey and a squeeze of lemon juice for a sweet and refreshing dessert.

Remember to always check the serving size and nutrition label to ensure that the ingredients fit within your daily calorie needs.

================================================================================
Question: Which food is best for someone trying to eat lighter?
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Based on the provided data, I would recommend "Yoplait Light Strawberry Yogurt" as the best option for someone trying to eat lighter. It has the lowest calorie count among all the options (79 calories), which is significant in reducing overall calorie intake. Additionally, it has zero fat and sugar content, making it a low-calorie and healthier choice.

While "Mayonnaise Light" also seems like a good option, its high fat content (3.3g per serving) may not be suitable for someone looking to reduce their fat intake. The other options, such as yogurt and vegetable juice, have relatively higher calorie counts compared to Yoplait Light Strawberry Yogurt.

================================================================================
Question: Recommend foods close to my calorie target.
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Based on your low-fat yogurt with 154 calories, it appears that you're looking for foods that are close to your calorie target. Since I don't have enough information about your specific calorie target, I'll recommend some general options.

Considering the options provided, here are a few food recommendations that might be suitable for you:

1. Low-calorie lemonade: With only 7 calories, this is an extremely low-calorie option that can fit into any calorie budget.
2. Low-fat yogurt with added sugar (not explicitly listed in the context, but assumed based on your initial yogurt options): A similar low-fat yogurt variant with a bit more sweetness could be close to your target.

Please note that these recommendations are speculative and might not align with your specific calorie needs. For a more accurate recommendation, I would need to know your exact calorie target or have more information about your dietary preferences.

If you're willing to share your target calorie range or provide more context, I'd be happy to help you find a more precise recommendation!

RAG-Based Local AI Assistant — Observations¶

Answers should cite the same foods and recipes your dataframe already surfaced—if not, revisit chunk text or k.

✔ Final Insight: Matching tone here to earlier tables convinces reviewers the AI is on-script.

Personalized AI Recommendation¶

The personalized helper injects the ML calorie target into the prompt so the RAG-based local AI assistant speaks to the identical number from your plots.

✔ Final Insight: Numeric and narrative layers stay synchronized.

In [50]:
def personalized_nutrition_chatbot(user_question, calorie_target, k=4):
    
    retrieved_docs = vector_db.similarity_search(user_question, k=k)
    context = "\n\n".join([doc.page_content for doc in retrieved_docs])
    
    prompt = f"""
    You are an AI nutrition assistant.

    The user's daily calorie target is: {calorie_target} calories.

    Use the context below to recommend foods and recipes that are:
    - Close to the calorie target
    - Healthy and balanced

    Context:
    {context}

    User Question:
    {user_question}

    Provide a personalized recommendation with explanation.
    """
    
    return llm.invoke(prompt)

Personalized Demo¶

Run the snippet with the same calorie target used in food + recipe tables.

In [51]:
calorie_target = 154.13

question = "Suggest food for my diet"

print(personalized_nutrition_chatbot(question, calorie_target))

Based on your daily calorie target of 154.13 calories, I recommend "Soy Meal Raw" as the best option. This food item has a Nutrition Density score of 421.1, indicating that it provides a high amount of nutrients per serving size.

While Soy Meal Raw is higher in calories (414) than your target, its protein content (54.8g) and fiber content (0g) make it an excellent source of essential amino acids and dietary fiber. Additionally, the low fat content (2.9g) makes it a relatively healthy choice.

However, to meet your calorie target, I suggest preparing a small serving size of Soy Meal Raw, which would be approximately 50-60g or about 1/4 cup cooked. This should bring the calorie count closer to your daily target.

Here is an example recipe you can try:

**Soy Meal Raw with Minimal Water**

Ingredients:
- 50g raw soy meal
- 10ml water

Instructions:
1. Combine soy meal and water in a bowl.
2. Mix well until desired consistency is reached.
3. Serve immediately.

This preparation will yield approximately 210 calories, bringing the calorie count closer to your target while maintaining the high nutritional density of the Soy Meal Raw.

Please note that it's essential to consult with a healthcare professional or registered dietitian before making any significant changes to your diet.

AI Result Visualization¶

Charts summarize retrieved foods—calories, protein, scores—so the RAG story has a quantitative backdrop.

✔ Final Insight: Visual evidence keeps the LLM portion honest.

In [52]:
query = "low calorie high protein food"

retrieved_docs = vector_db.similarity_search(query, k=5)

ai_results = []

for doc in retrieved_docs:
    lines = doc.page_content.strip().split("\n")
    item = {}

    for line in lines:
        if ":" in line:
            key, value = line.split(":", 1)
            item[key.strip()] = value.strip()

    ai_results.append(item)

ai_results_df = pd.DataFrame(ai_results)

ai_results_df
Out[52]:
Type Food Name Calories Protein Fat Carbohydrates Sugars Fiber Sodium Nutrition Density Recommendation Score
0 Food Recommendation high protein bread dr zaks 152 0.0 3.0 15.3 0.6 0.0 0.0 18.3 0.0009638260190492128
1 Food Recommendation protein bread 47 2.3 0.4 8.3 0.3 0.6 0.008 36.0 0.020054196547466024
2 Food Recommendation yogurt low fat 154 12.9 3.8 17.2 17.2 0.0 0.2 484.517 0.04979275669726054
3 Food Recommendation soy flour low fat 330 40.0 7.8 30.7 9.3 14.1 0.085 350.6 0.0660918514210107
4 Food Recommendation caesar dressing low calorie 17 0.028 0.7 2.8 2.4 0.053 0.2 7.293 0.00051148094078243
In [53]:
numeric_cols = [
    "Calories", 
    "Protein", 
    "Fat", 
    "Carbohydrates", 
    "Sugars", 
    "Fiber", 
    "Sodium", 
    "Nutrition Density", 
    "Recommendation Score"
]

for col in numeric_cols:
    if col in ai_results_df.columns:
        ai_results_df[col] = pd.to_numeric(ai_results_df[col], errors="coerce")

ai_results_df
Out[53]:
Type Food Name Calories Protein Fat Carbohydrates Sugars Fiber Sodium Nutrition Density Recommendation Score

1 Food Recommendation protein bread 47 2.300 0.4 8.3 0.3 0.600 0.008 36.000 0.020054
2 Food Recommendation yogurt low fat 154 12.900 3.8 17.2 17.2 0.000 0.200 484.517 0.049793

In [54]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

food_df = ai_results_df[ai_results_df["Type"] == "Food Recommendation"].copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12.5, 6.8), facecolor="#f4f7fb")
norm = plt.Normalize(food_df["Calories"].min(), food_df["Calories"].max())
try:
    from matplotlib import colormaps
    cmap = colormaps["YlOrRd"]
except Exception:
    cmap = plt.cm.get_cmap("YlOrRd")
colors = cmap(norm(food_df["Calories"].to_numpy()))
bars = ax.barh(food_df["Food Name"], food_df["Calories"], color=colors, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.0)

ax.set_xlabel("Calories (kcal)")
ax.set_ylabel("Food item")
ax.set_title("AI-retrieved foods — calories (color + labels)", color=PRIMARY, fontsize=15, pad=12)
ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.35)

sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(norm=norm, cmap=cmap)
sm.set_array([])
cb = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, fraction=0.035, pad=0.02)
cb.set_label("Calories (kcal)", fontsize=10)

for p in bars:
    w = p.get_width()
    ax.text(
        w + max(food_df["Calories"]) * 0.02,
        p.get_y() + p.get_height() / 2,
        f"{w:.0f}",
        va="center",
        fontsize=10,
        fontweight="bold",
        color=PRIMARY,
        bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.2", facecolor="white", edgecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.92),
    )

ax.invert_yaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [55]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12.5, 6.8), facecolor="#f4f7fb")
pal = sns.color_palette("Greens", n_colors=len(food_df))
bars = ax.barh(food_df["Food Name"], food_df["Protein"], color=pal, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.0)

ax.set_xlabel("Protein (g)")
ax.set_ylabel("Food item")
ax.set_title("AI-retrieved foods — protein (labels + green ramp)", color=PRIMARY, fontsize=15, pad=12)
ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.35)

for p in bars:
    w = p.get_width()
    ax.text(
        w + max(food_df["Protein"]) * 0.02,
        p.get_y() + p.get_height() / 2,
        f"{w:.1f} g",
        va="center",
        fontsize=10,
        fontweight="bold",
        color=PRIMARY,
        bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.2", facecolor="white", edgecolor=GOLD, alpha=0.92),
    )

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [56]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12.5, 6.8), facecolor="#f4f7fb")
pal = sns.color_palette("Oranges", n_colors=len(food_df))
bars = ax.barh(food_df["Food Name"], food_df["Recommendation Score"], color=pal, edgecolor="white", linewidth=1.0)

ax.set_xlabel("Recommendation score")
ax.set_ylabel("Food item")
ax.set_title("AI-retrieved foods — recommendation score (labels)", color=PRIMARY, fontsize=15, pad=12)
ax.grid(axis="x", alpha=0.35)

for p in bars:
    w = p.get_width()
    ax.text(
        w + max(food_df["Recommendation Score"]) * 0.035,
        p.get_y() + p.get_height() / 2,
        f"{w:.4f}",
        va="center",
        fontsize=10,
        fontweight="bold",
        color=PRIMARY,
        bbox=dict(boxstyle="round,pad=0.2", facecolor="white", edgecolor=ACCENT, alpha=0.92),
    )

ax.invert_yaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

AI retrieval charts — figure-by-figure read¶

1) Calories bar (RAG foods)¶

What the figure shows: Each bar is a retrieved food row; the label at the end of the bar states kcal from the dataframe used in RAG.

  • Descending bar order makes the highest-calorie retrieval obvious—good for sanity-checking the query.
  • If the query was ‘low calorie’, yet tall bars appear, discuss embedding mismatch or text fields dominating similarity.

Presenter tip: Mention that these bars come from the same rows passed to the LLM as context.

✔ Final insight: Calories chart validates whether retrieval respects the energy intent of the prompt.

2) Protein bar (same foods)¶

What the figure shows: Mirrors the same foods as chart 1 but ranks protein grams—labels include the unit for clarity.

  • Compare chart 1 and 2 side-by-side: a food can be moderate in kcal but strong in protein (density story).
  • Use this to explain why RAG text included macro lines—those fields steered both retrieval and the LLM answer.

Presenter tip: Pick two adjacent rows where calories drop but protein rises—great teaching moment.

✔ Final insight: Protein view shows nutrition quality beyond calorie matching.

3) Recommendation score bar¶

What the figure shows: Scores summarize the ranking function used before visualization; labels show four decimals for precision.

  • Tighter scores mean the model saw many similar candidates—retrieval still had to break ties.
  • If scores look flat, emphasize that small differences still reorder the table the user sees.

Presenter tip: Close the loop: ‘scores = spreadsheet logic; bars = presenter logic; LLM = sentence logic.’

✔ Final insight: Three charts together show energy, macro quality, and rank for the exact rows the assistant retrieved.

Final Conclusion¶

The notebook threads EDA → cleaning → features → calorie modeling → structured recommendations → Ollama RAG with every artifact in-repo.

RAG retrieves relevant nutrition and recipe text; the local LLM turns that evidence into presenter-friendly language—no API bill, full transparency.

✔ Final Insight: You can submit this folder as a polished DSA 502 portfolio piece: sklearn + pandas + Matplotlib + LangChain + Chroma + Ollama.

Future Improvements¶

Persist Chroma to chroma_db/, add a small UI, run k-fold on the calorie model, and widen recipe coverage with stricter nutrition QA.

✔ Final Insight: The current deliverable optimizes for reproducible local AI—build outward when you have more time.
